content.problemStatement:
Perhaps you know about the famous Josephus problem where some hapless people are standing in a circle waiting to be executed. Here, our problem is a little bit different in which computational efficiency is more important.

After losing a battle, nnn soldiers, with unique integer IDs 1,2,…,n1, 2, \ldots, n1,2,…,n are standing in a circle. Counting from 1 (The soldier with ID 1 is counted 1) every kthk^{th}kth (1≤k≤10001 \leq k \leq 10001≤k≤1000) soldier is taken to prison and later executed, and from the next soldier counting goes on until just one soldier survives. Find the ID of the last soldier taken to prison (the soldier who was taken to the prison immediately before the last surviving soldier).


Input Format

For each instance of the problem, there will be a single line of input which contains two space separated integers nnn and kkk.
Input ends with a line containing two zeros.
There will be maximum 120 such instances.
For all instances, 2≤n≤10122 \leq n \leq 10^{12}2≤n≤1012 and 1≤k≤10001 \leq k \leq 10001≤k≤1000.


Output Format

For each instance your program should output the ID of the last soldier taken to prison in a single line.
content.inputFormat: The input consists of multiple test cases. Each test case is given on a new line and contains two space-separated integers n and k, where n is the number of soldiers and k is the interval at which soldiers are taken to prison. The input ends with a line containing two zeros.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the ID of the last soldier taken to prison in a single line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the soldiers are arranged in a circle and counted from 1. The soldiers are taken to prison at intervals of 3. The position of the last soldier taken to prison is calculated using the formula (n - (n - 1) % k) % n + 1, which gives (10 - (10 - 1) % 3) % 10 + 1 = 10. For the second test case, the soldiers are arranged in a circle and counted from 1. The soldiers are taken to prison at intervals of 2. The position of the last soldier taken to prison is calculated using the formula (n - (n - 1) % k) % n + 1, which gives (4 - (4 - 1) % 2) % 4 + 1 = 3.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a recursive approach based on the Josephus problem formula. The formula states that the position of the last soldier taken to prison can be calculated using the formula f(n, k) = (f(n - 1, k) + k - 1) % n + 1, where f(n, k) is the position of the last soldier taken to prison for n soldiers and interval k. However, since the problem involves large values of n, we need to use a more efficient approach. We can use the fact that the position of the last soldier taken to prison is the same as the position of the soldier who would be at position (n - 1) if the soldiers were arranged in a circle and counted from 1. Therefore, we can calculate the position of the last soldier taken to prison using the formula (n - (n - 1) % k) % n + 1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(1) - The time complexity is constant because we are only performing a constant number of operations to calculate the position of the last soldier taken to prison.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) - The space complexity is constant because we are only using a constant amount of space to store the input values and the result.

code.Python3:
import sys
for line in sys.stdin:
    n, k = map(int, line.split())
    if n == 0 and k == 0: break
    print((n - (n - 1) % k) % n + 1)
